title: ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties — Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.5.3 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.3 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.8: ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties — Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.8' running on index=_audit (object_type, action, user, object, info), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=_audit` (object_type, action, user, object, info) (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
spl:
index=_audit object_type IN ("savedsearch","alert_actions","transforms","props","authorize") action IN ("create","update","delete") earliest=-30d
| eval object_name=coalesce(object, info)
| stats count as changes, values(action) as actions, dc(object_type) as object_types by user
| lookup splunk_privileged_users.csv user OUTPUT is_breakglass
| where is_breakglass="true" OR changes>25
| sort - changes
description: Conflicting duties must be separated to reduce fraud and error. Auditing edits to high-risk Splunk objects by the same accounts that can execute destructive searches evidences compensating detective controls where native SoD is limited.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes _audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `splunk_privileged_users.csv` for admin and break-glass IDs; (2) forward `_audit` from all search heads in the cluster; (3) alert on deletes to `authorize` or `props`; (4) pair with change tickets via `user`→`snow_sys_id` lookup; (5) include in role recertification for A.5.3 (segregation of duties). Indexes required: `index=_audit`.
visualization: Table (user, changes, actions), Timeline (edits by object_type), Single value (delete actions count).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Emergency admin operations** — Privileged operations performed during declared incidents using break-glass procedures are documented in incident records.

2. **Knowledge object migration activities** — Bulk knowledge object changes during app migrations or upgrades are pre-approved through the change advisory board.

3. **Automated CI/CD deployments** — Deployment pipelines that modify knowledge objects as part of approved release processes operate under service account segregation.

4. **Shared admin role during staff transitions** — Brief periods where admin duties overlap during personnel changes are within the documented transition plan.

5. **Development environment exceptions** — Non-production Splunk instances where segregation of duties requirements are relaxed per the risk acceptance framework.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `_audit` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `splunk_privileged_users.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('splunk_privileged_users.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.5.3`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.3 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.8: ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties — Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `_audit` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain `splunk_privileged_users.csv` for admin and break-glass IDs. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** forward `_audit` from all search heads in the cluster. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert on deletes to `authorize` or `props`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** pair with change tickets via `user`→`snow_sys_id` lookup. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** include in role recertification for A.5.3 (segregation of duties). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties — Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes," the aggregation exposes change activity — change frequency, change authorship, and change surface are the dimensions the regulation cares about, not raw events.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `splunk_privileged_users.csv` — the identity register that maps accounts to real users and roles. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`is_breakglass`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `user`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `splunk_privileged_users.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval user = lower(user)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 30 days.

Numeric threshold `changes > 25`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.8: ISO 27001 Segregation of Duties — Privileged Splunk Knowledg]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.8,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `actions`, `changes`, `object_name`, `object_types`, `user`. Run:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table actions, changes, object_name, object_types, user
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `splunk_privileged_users.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup splunk_privileged_users.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `changes`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(changes) as p50 perc90(changes) as p90 perc99(changes) as p99 max(changes) as hi
```

The current threshold `25` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.8: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`user`, `changes`, `actions`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`edits by object_type`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`delete actions count`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'ISO 27001 current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Segregation of Duties — Privileged Splunk Knowledge Object Changes. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Grant a test user elevated access to a controlled system without a corresponding approval record in the access management system; verify the monitoring search detects the unapproved privilege within 1 hour and generates an alert with user, system, and missing approval details.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Grant a test user access following the full approval workflow with manager sign-off, risk assessment, and time-limited duration recorded; verify no access violation alert fires and the access appears as properly authorised in the dashboard.

evidence: Access governance report showing recertification completion rates, privilege changes, approval chain documentation, and anomalous access investigation outcomes.
exclusions: Approved maintenance window access; automated service account operations per register; break-glass access during declared incidents; access within recertification cycle timing; project-based temporary elevation.
